title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption: Between 1950 and 2100, period life expectancy at birth follows distinct upward trajectories for Japan, Sweden, Brazil, India, and the global average, with solid lines marking observed data through 2020 and dotted lines extending UN medium-variant projections to the end of the century. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 100 years, while the horizontal axis covers seven decades of historical record and eight decades of forecast. In 1950, India begins near 41 years, the lowest starting point among the named entities, while Japan starts around 60 years and Sweden near 70 years; the world average sits just under 50 years. By 2020, Japan reaches approximately 84 years, Sweden holds steady near 83 years, Brazil climbs to about 76 years, India approaches 70 years, and the global average crosses 72 years. A visible dip occurs in India’s line around 2020, dropping briefly before resuming its ascent in the projection phase. Projected to 2100, Japan extends beyond 92 years, Sweden nears 90 years, Brazil reaches roughly 86 years, India approaches 84 years, and the world average settles near 82 years. Throughout the entire span, Japan maintains the highest life expectancy after overtaking Sweden in the early 2000s, while India shows the steepest gain over time, narrowing the gap with higher-income nations. The lines show consistent growth across all series, with no reversal or plateau visible in the projected period, and the spacing between them contracts gradually as lower-starting countries catch up toward the upper range.
